title: Low-cost Ultra-low Noise DAC System-on-Module for Scalable Ion-Trap Electrode Control
authors: Mitchell G. Peaks, Mia M. Kaarls, Crystal Noel
abstract: A new design for an open-hardware Digital-to-Analog Converter System-on-Module is presented for low-noise ion-trap electrode control. The design specifications were established to fill the technical needs of a modular, scalable DC electrode control platform with sufficient bandwidth, noise characteristics and control flexibility. Critically, a priority was placed on supply-chain management considerations and cost effectiveness for scaling. The system is based upon the Texas Instruments DAC81416 and AMD Xilinx Spartan-7 FPGA for the analog signal and compute architecture respectively. Performance characterization of a prototype device suggests the design is suitable for a variety of ion-trap physics experiments and quantum computing applications.
summary: The paper presents the design of a low-cost, open-hardware DAC System-on-Module for precise control of ion-trap electrodes. It addresses the challenges of scalability and supply-chain security by using widely available components to achieve ultra-low noise performance. The resulting prototype is a viable, extensible platform for large-scale quantum computing applications.
main_problem: The need for a scalable, low-cost, and ultra-low noise DC electrode control system for ion-trap quantum technologies that avoids proprietary ecosystems and supply-chain vulnerabilities.
main_result: The development of the 'Vanguard DAC,' an open-hardware System-on-Module that achieves ultra-low noise performance (comparable to ground plane noise) and high-precision voltage control suitable for ion-trap applications.
method: A mixed-signal PCB design utilizing a Texas Instruments DAC81416 and an AMD Xilinx Spartan-7 FPGA, featuring a two-stage linear voltage regulation strategy and an 8-layer stack-up for EMI mitigation.
model_or_system: An open-hardware Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC) System-on-Module (SoM) designed for controlling DC electrodes in trapped ion quantum computing, simulators, and sensors.
key_observables: Output noise density, power spectral density (PSD), voltage resolution (LSB), slew rate, settling time, and crosstalk between channels.
important_parameters: 16-bit resolution, +/-10 V output range, 50 MHz clock frequency, 48.22 kHz RC filter cutoff, and 1.76 V/us measured slew rate.
main_assumptions: The design assumes that using OEM components and open-hardware architecture can effectively mitigate risks of component obsolescence and proprietary lock-in.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows design requirements; Figure 2 presents CAD models of the SoM; Figure 3 shows PCB layout details; Figure 4 illustrates the control infrastructure; Figure 5 and 6 show voltage accuracy and inter-device offsets; Figure 7 displays the power spectral density; Figure 8 shows voltage transition waveforms.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the design requirements, describes the hardware architecture and power management, details the PCB engineering and implementation, presents performance characterization (noise, accuracy, and transient response), and concludes with future improvements.
why_interesting: This provides the essential high-precision hardware infrastructure required to implement complex ion-shuttling and transport protocols in large-scale QCCD architectures, which is critical for scaling up ion-trap quantum computers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 2: CAD model of the PCB design for v0.1 Revision A Mk1 Vanguard System-on-Module.

Fig. 3: Close-up of the PCB design showing the TI DAC81416 footprints, DAC output traces connecting to the external con- nector pads, and the EMI wall and stitching vias placed across the board to prevent EMI, channel cross-talk and to stitch the PCB ground planes.

Fig. 4: Diagrammatic representation of the minimal control infrastructure build for testing the SoM. The communication protocols are delineated in blue, and the hardware modules which handle communication are implemented in FPGA fab- ric.

Fig. 5: Measurements taken at a sample of the output channels (samples taken from each power register) for a set of 16-bit word register write values corresponding to a 16-bit division of the DAC output range (-10 V to +10 V). ∆Voltage represents the difference between the target value in the legend and the measured value. The legend gives rounded values of the nominal code voltage correspondence for reference purposes.
